Adapters efficiently fine-tune models on specific tasks with minimal parameter changes, reducing computational and storage requirements while maintaining similar performance to full fine-tuning.

Our study focuses on retrieving and merging multiple Low-Rank adapters (LoRA), the popular approach within existing open-source communities. Adapter composition has emerged as a crucial mechanism for enhancing the capabilities of foundational models across various applications.
For large language models (LLM), the linear combination of multiple adapters improves in-domain performance and cross-task generalization.
Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is the dominant approach for the image domain, merging LoRAs effectively composes different tasks—concepts.

Adapters play a key role in synthetic data methods in few-shot computer vision. Our approach advances this further by actively segmenting user prompts into distinct tasks and merging the appropriate adapters for each task.
Retrieval-based methods, such as RAG, significantly improve model responses by adding semantically similar texts from a vast external database.

Our work draws inspiration from RAG to encode adapters as vector embeddings: leveraging visual-language foundational models (VLM) to generate semantic descriptions of adapters, which are then translated into embeddings.

Large adapter weights applied to a single task can introduce visual artifacts, such as over-saturation.
Adapters are designed to modify distinct, orthogonal concepts, resulting in orthogonal weights across tasks.
The final merged model weights are computed by averaging weights per task and summing weights across tasks from adapter weights.
The refiner outputs recommended weight/coefficient for each adapter which are then normalized by task to sum up the adapter weights for the final merged model.
The model weights are merged by summing the adapter weights normalized by task. The relationship between adapter weights and the task is crucial in determining the final model weight.
High adapter weights assigned to an individual task can lead to sharp decreases in image quality, as mentioned in App. A.4.

Stylus is a flexible algorithm that automatically selects and composes adapters to generate better images.
Stylus leverages a three-stage framework that precomputes adapters as lookup embeddings and retrieves the most relevant adapters based on prompts' keywords.
To evaluate Stylus, StylusDocs, a processed dataset featuring 75K adapters and pre-computed adapter embeddings, is developed.
Evaluation of Stylus across automatic metrics, humans, and vision-language models, demonstrate that Stylus achieves better visual fidelity compared to existing Stable Diffusion checkpoints.
Evaluation of Stylus across automatic metrics, humans, and vision-language models, demonstrate that Stylus achieves better textual alignment compared to existing Stable Diffusion checkpoints.
Evaluation of Stylus across automatic metrics, humans, and vision-language models, demonstrate that Stylus achieves better image diversity compared to existing Stable Diffusion checkpoints.
